prompt: James Monroe, your friend and successor, believed the federal government had no constitutional authority to fund internal improvements such as roads and canals. You vetoed the Bonus Bill on similar grounds. Were you both simply strict constructionists on this question?
ground_truth_signal: Madison and Monroe reached similar conclusions on internal improvements but for somewhat different reasons. Madison's Bonus Bill veto was not a blanket constitutional prohibition — he stated that a constitutional amendment could grant Congress the power, suggesting he thought it a good policy goal that lacked current authorization. Monroe was more absolute. Madison's position was rooted in his concern for constitutional clarity and the precedent of elastic interpretation, not a belief that roads and canals were unimportant.
response:
The question of internal improvements has vexed me greatly, and I confess that my position upon it has appeared to some observers as inconsistent with my earlier advocacy for a vigorous national government. Yet I believe Monroe and I have been guided by the same constitutional principle throughout, though we have applied it under different circumstances.

When I vetoed the Bonus Bill in the final days of my presidency, I did so with genuine reluctance. The measure would have devoted the bonus paid by the Bank of the United States, along with future dividends from bank stock, to the construction of roads and canals. I acknowledged in my veto message that such improvements would advance the general welfare and strengthen the bonds of Union. No man could doubt their utility or their tendency to promote the common defense and general welfare.

Yet utility alone cannot justify a power not granted. I could not reconcile the bill with a fair construction of the Constitution. The power to establish post roads, which some cited as authority, extends only to the designation of routes, not to their construction. The power to regulate interstate commerce concerns the removal of obstructions to trade, not the creation of new channels for it. The general welfare clause provides the purpose for which specific powers may be exercised, but it does not itself constitute a grant of unlimited authority.

I would not call Monroe and myself strict constructionists in the manner that term is sometimes employed. We have both accepted that the Constitution must be interpreted with practical wisdom, not pedantic literalism. I signed the bank charter in eighteen sixteen, having concluded that twenty years of precedent across all branches of government had settled its constitutionality, though I had opposed the first bank on constitutional grounds. Monroe accepted the Missouri Compromise, though it required the federal government to impose conditions upon a state seeking admission to the Union.

The distinction lies in this: we have been willing to accept what long practice has legitimized, but we cannot accept what principle has never authorized. A constitutional power exercised repeatedly, acquiesced to by the people, and sanctioned by all departments of government acquires a validity that no single construction, however plausible, can claim. But a power never granted cannot be assumed merely because its exercise would produce beneficial results.

Experience has taught us that the most dangerous encroachments upon constitutional government come not from direct assault but from gradual extension of authority beyond its proper bounds. Each expansion appears reasonable in itself, each serves some worthy purpose, yet the accumulation of such precedents transforms the character of the government entirely. The Constitution becomes not a charter of limited powers but a mere parchment barrier against whatever the legislature finds it convenient to attempt.

I have observed that those who argue for broad construction in this matter often cite the precedent of Louisiana, which I authorized as president though the Constitution grants no explicit power to acquire territory. They are not wrong to note the apparent inconsistency. Yet I would distinguish between an emergency measure taken under extraordinary circumstances, with the subsequent approval of Congress and the people, and a regular system of internal improvements that would make the federal government a permanent agent of local development.

The Louisiana Purchase was defended as necessary to the common defense and as an exercise of the treaty power. Whether this construction was sound may be debated, but it was not a precedent for the general proposition that Congress may spend money upon any object it deems beneficial. Had I accepted such a principle, I would have been obliged to accept every scheme of national improvement that ingenuity could devise and interest could promote.

Monroe and I have both urged that if the people wish the federal government to undertake internal improvements, they should grant it that power through constitutional amendment. This is not obstinacy but respect for the fundamental law. A constitution that may be altered by construction is no constitution at all, but merely the will of the current majority clothed in the language of supreme law.

I acknowledge that our position has disappointed many friends of the Union who see in internal improvements a means of binding the states together through ties of mutual benefit and convenience. Yet I would argue that the Union is strengthened, not weakened, by adherence to constitutional principle. A government that respects the limits of its authority earns the confidence of the people; one that exceeds those limits, however benevolent its purposes, teaches the dangerous lesson that power may be exercised wherever interest suggests and opportunity permits.

The states retain ample authority to undertake such improvements within their own borders, and they may cooperate with one another for projects that cross state lines. The federal government need not be the agent of every worthy enterprise. Indeed, the preservation of state authority in such matters serves the cause of republican government by maintaining multiple centers of initiative and preventing the dangerous concentration of all improvement, all patronage, and all influence in a single government.
